nodes.name: 栖息地破坏
nodes.summary: 开发、采伐和围垦等活动导致适宜生境减少。

nodes.name: LC
nodes.summary: LC表示 IUCN 无危等级，当前关联8208种鸟类，如鸵鸟、鸸鹋、南方鶴鴕等8208种鸟类。

nodes.name: 公园
nodes.summary: 公园是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联2种鸟类，包括鵰鴞、New Zealand bellbird，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 班克斯半島
nodes.summary: 班克斯半島是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联3种鸟类，包括Hutton's shearwater、黄眼企鹅、New Zealand bellbird，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 灌丛
nodes.summary: 低矮木本植物占优势的陆地生境。

nodes.name: New Zealand bellbird
nodes.summary: New Zealand bellbird（New Zealand bellbird，学名 Anthornis melanura），在图谱中标记为无危（LC）物种，主要分布于班克斯半島、公园，常见栖息地包括灌丛，主要威胁包括外来捕食者、森林砍伐、栖息地破坏。

nodes.name: 外来捕食者
nodes.summary: 岛屿鸟类常受猫、鼬和鼠等外来种影响。

nodes.name: 森林砍伐
nodes.summary: 原生林被砍伐导致栖息地面积和连通性下降。

links.label: 分布于
links.evidence: |last3=Ji |first3=Weihong |date=2008 |title=Assessing natural dispersal of New Zealand bellbirds using song type and song playbacks |url=https://newzealandecology.org/nzje/2872 |journal=New Zealand Journal of Ecology |volume=32 |issue=2 |pages=147–154 |ref=Brunton2008}} left|thumb|Male bellbird on a kōwhai tree, [[Banks Peninsula]] The bellbird's habitat encompasses both native and exotic forests and scrublands, and it is frequently found in urban areas such as parks and gardens, especially if there is a nearby patch of native bush

links.label: 分布于
links.evidence: |last3=Ji |first3=Weihong |date=2008 |title=Assessing natural dispersal of New Zealand bellbirds using song type and song playbacks |url=https://newzealandecology.org/nzje/2872 |journal=New Zealand Journal of Ecology |volume=32 |issue=2 |pages=147–154 |ref=Brunton2008}} left|thumb|Male bellbird on a kōwhai tree, [[Banks Peninsula]] The bellbird's habitat encompasses both native and exotic forests and scrublands, and it is frequently found in urban areas such as parks and gardens, especially if there is a nearby patch of native bush

links.label: 栖息于
links.evidence: Its habitat includes both native and exotic forests and scrublands, and it is commonly found in parks and gardens

links.label: 受威胁于
links.evidence: At the time of European arrival, they were one of the most common birds. Bellbird populations have declined significantly due to the destruction of native forests (initiated by Māori and perpetuated by Europeans) and the introduction of invasive predators such as cats (Felis catus), mustelids including ferrets (Mustela furo), stoats (M

links.label: 受威胁于
links.evidence: nivalis), and rodents such as the brown rat (Rattus norvegicus) and the black rat (Rattus rattus). Between the 1860s and about 1900, bellbird numbers declined very dramatically, prompting many ornithologists to search for reasons beyond deforestation and introduced mammals. Walter Buller, the prominent New Zealand ornithologist of the 19th century, in 1871 quoted Hokianga resident Frederick Maning saying: The decline started in the north of the country and moved south. By 1870, bellbirds had vanished from Northland, and between 1868 and 1878 they disappeared from Great Barrier Island. In the South Island, bellbird numbers in Canterbury did not reach their lowest point until 1900

links.label: 受威胁于
links.evidence: |date=2021 |title=Conservation status of birds in Aotearoa New Zealand, 2021 |url=https://www.doc.govt.nz/globalassets/documents/science-and-technical/nztcs36entire.pdf |website=Department of Conservation Te Papa Atawhai}} According to IUCN and a 2010 study, the overall bellbird population is suspected to be in decline as a result of ongoing habitat destruction and predation by introduced mammals. The decline is most prominent in lowland areas under 1000 metres in elevation. A 2022 study of New Zealand garden birds concluded that bellbird numbers are stable or locally increasing.{{Cite journal |last1=MacLeod |first1=Catriona J
